channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: NATO’s Waiting Game
item.description: Less Trump. More Europe. What’s next?

item.title: U.S., Iran Return to the Brink of All-Out War
item.description: As both sides intensify strikes, future peace talks appear uncertain.

item.title: Can Indo-Pacific Powers Deter China Without the US?
item.description: The Indo-Pacific is dead; long live the Indo-Pacific.

item.title: Will Iraq’s New Prime Minister Really Take On Corruption?
item.description: Ali al-Zaidi tackles a perpetual challenge under the watchful eyes of Iraqis and Washington.

item.title: An Athenian View of American Democracy
item.description: At 250, America is now producing a mindless, raw power and exponentially growing instability. The possibility of catharsis that characterized the United States at its bicentennial is hard to envisage a half-century later—to the detriment of us all.

item.title: What Did China’s Missile Test Have to Do With the Marco Polo Bridge Incident?
item.description: A CASC poster connected the recent SLBM launch to the start of the Second Sino-Japanese War.

item.title: A World on Fire Needs Australia’s Conflict Mediation Prowess
item.description: Australia can help itself and its region by burnishing its credentials as a trusted international mediator.

item.title: Low-Cost Solutions to Maternal Mortality Already Exist
item.description: As foreign aid declines, policymakers in lower-income countries must increasingly focus on making better use of scarce resources. When it comes to maternal mortality, that means investing in simple, proven, and affordable interventions for postpartum hemorrhage, pre-eclampsia, and obstructed labor.

item.title: Why Is the India-US Bilateral Trade Agreement Still On Hold?
item.description: Conflicting domestic economic policies, sudden judicial disruptions, and fresh American tariff investigations have upended the India-U.S. trade deal.

item.title: The world is making heady progress in the fight against dementia
item.description: Incidence among the elderly is falling fast

item.title: Who is capable of evil?
item.description: Stop lowering the age of criminal responsibility

item.title: A no-brainer for protecting your brain
item.description: One simple vaccination may dramatically reduce the risk of dementia

item.title: England needs fewer council homes, not more
item.description: Andy Burnham’s plan is no way to ease the housing crisis

item.title: Two cheers for Trump Accounts
item.description: The grubby scheme contains the seeds of a good idea

item.title: How South Korean Buddhists are trying to stay relevant
item.description: Life is suffering; the Buddha can help

item.title: Laos has big dreams of exporting energy
item.description: But its dams have flooded it with debt and other problems

item.title: China’s media gloat at Britain’s chaotic democracy
item.description: But Chinese viewers also love British comedy that laughs at politics

item.title: China releases a prominent Christian pastor
item.description: Jin Mingri formerly ran a large house church in Beijing

item.title: Child suspects are being prosecuted at younger ages
item.description: Some governments are lowering the age of criminal responsibility. That may backfire

item.title: The left’s common-sense problem on crime
item.description: Radical ideas may seriously harm the Democratic Party

item.title: Texas is waging a battle to stop “Islamification”
item.description: America’s biggest Republican state offers a playbook for a new identity politics

item.title: Why Donald Trump won’t let a doomed bill die
item.description: The SAVE Act is unlikely to pass Congress. Donald Trump may prefer it that way

item.title: Rise of the Gen-Z Luddite
item.description: Resistance to tech is brewing among digital natives

item.title: Cartoon: The real threat to global growth
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Business
Politics
Europe’s public broadcasters go from prime time to hard-to-find
item.description: Apathetic teenagers and culture warriors are hobbling venerable state media outlets

item.title: Poland and Ukraine have fallen out over history
item.description: The row could have lasting consequences for Europe’s security

item.title: The secret to good questions
item.description: Consideration, consistency and curveballs

item.title: Could a Chinese bike win the Tour de France?
item.description: The country’s manufacturers hope that high-performance models will improve their image

item.title: Ahmed al-Sharaa is quashing Syria’s vast captagon trade
item.description: But pills that cost cents to produce still sell for $25

item.title: Can Africa take the Asian path to growth?
item.description: Stalling agricultural productivity makes it look unlikely

item.title: Americans and Canadians are squabbling over mines
item.description: The frontier complicates disagreements between indigenous groups

item.title: India’s Indo-Pacific Strategy Is a Search for Options, Not Substitutes
item.description: New Delhi wants more capitals it can call on, not fewer.

item.title: Postmodern Economics
item.description: Eschewing the idea of a single truth, the recently deceased English artist David Hockney showed that the world unfolds before us in ways we cannot fully understand. After long clinging to the pretense of certainty, economists would do well to embrace the same insight.

item.title: Indian mangoes have been banned from Japan
item.description: It’s the latest sign of a widespread food-safety problem

item.title: Ukraine Finally Has a Theory of Victory. Will It Work?
item.description: Kyiv has developed a strategy for winning the war. Now all it needs is time.

item.title: Digital Public Infrastructure Is Now a Strategic Battleground
item.description: Digital identity platforms, payment systems, and data exchanges promise more efficient, inclusive, and responsive public services. But in an era of geopolitical rivalry and concentrated digital power, those same systems create strategic dependencies that governments can no longer afford to ignore.

item.title: China may struggle to fund Xi Jinping’s tech ambitions
item.description: The comeback capital markets are staging may not be enough

item.title: China may struggle to fund Xi Jinping’s tech dreams
item.description: Even though capital markets are staging a comeback

item.title: An ageing society might not cost too much
item.description: A dystopian vision of the future may not come to pass

item.title: Should the Bank of Japan Raise Interest Rates?
item.description: At the beginning of 2013, when "Abenomics" was rolled out in Japan, the yen was significantly overvalued, and looser monetary policy was badly needed. Today, the opposite is true, indicating that interest-rate hikes may well be the right prescription for Japan.

item.title: Ayodhya’s Ram Temple, Narendra Modi’s Pet Project, is Mired in Corruption
item.description: Devotees’ donations have allegedly been siphoned away under the watch of Sangh Parivar leaders. With elections in Uttar Pradesh due next year, the BJP has swung into damage control mode.

item.title: India’s Organized Crime is No Longer Merely an Indian Problem
item.description: An Indian organized crime syndicate led by Lawrence Bishnoi is being treated as a transnational security threat requiring coordinated international action.

item.title: The man who would change Russia
item.description: A leading oligarch speaks out, warning of the looming disaster facing his country

item.title: Trump Is Remaking Latin America
item.description: The short-term results and the long-term risks of the “Donroe Doctrine.”

item.title: How to Save the UN From Irrelevance
item.description: Ending the peacekeeper’s paralysis.

item.title: How Southeast Asia’s Tourism Sector Changed in 2025
item.description: Thailand remains the biggest regional draw for foreign tourists, but the sector is undergoing some interesting and potentially significant shifts.

item.title: Cost of Car Ownership in Singapore Hits a New Record High
item.description: The price of a "certificate for entitlement" for a small vehicle rose to nearly $100,000 in the latest round of bidding.

item.title: How Cambodia Can Fix Its Tourism Problem
item.description: The country can learn a lot from its neighbors about how to sell itself in a competitive international market.

item.title: India’s PM Winds Up Visit to Indonesia, Hails ‘New Avenues’ For Cooperation
item.description: The two sides signed 14 agreements, including a landmark deal for the provision of BrahMos supersonic missiles to the Indonesian armed forces.

item.title: NATO survives its bipolar summit
item.description: Allies breath a sigh of relief; Ukraine may even cheer

item.title: 1 Year On, Cambodia Is Still Feeling the Impact of USAID’s Demise
item.description: To many in the country's development sector, the sudden collapse of the U.S. aid agency felt “like an earthquake.”
